coaching.coaching: Pin down concrete verification command for AGENTS.md pointer AC with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: concrete verification command for AGENTS.md pointer AC

coaching.coaching: Weigh at least one structural alternative for missing-file error hint fitness for author guides.
coaching.subject: missing-file error hint fitness for author guides

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to AC4, e.g. `grep -E 'writing-a-passing-plan|passing-code-review' AGENTS.md` (exit 0) and `grep -v 'plan-review-criteria-guide' AGENTS.md` (or equivalent negative grep), so the pointer fix is verifiable in-session.
evidence: AC4 text: 'AGENTS.md points authors at both new docs; no pointer claims the template lives in the criterion registry' — no proving command follows
All other ACs with doc-content requirements pair them with at least 'reading the file and rebar explain plan printing it (exit 0)'; AC4 provides neither
findings.finding: AC4 ('AGENTS.md points authors at both new docs; no pointer claims the template lives in the criterion registry') has no concrete proving command. The AC states an observable outcome but provides no in-session check (e.g. a grep or diff) to verify the pointer was fixed and the incorrect claim was removed.
scenarios: An executor who verifies only by running the listed commands would have no automated or semi-automated signal that the AGENTS.md pointers were actually updated correctly.
A reviewer cannot confirm the incorrect pointer was removed without knowing what to grep for.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC4: 'verified by `grep -E "writing-a-passing-plan|passing-code-review" AGENTS.md` exiting 0 and `grep -c "plan-review-criteria-guide" AGENTS.md` returning 0 (no remaining mispointer).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `missing-file` recovery hint for `explain_guide` (distinct from the generated-guide regeneration command) so the error message is actionable when a hand-authored doc is absent from the repo.
evidence: Existing ExplainError missing-file message: 'run `python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry regenerate-criteria-guide`' (registry.py line 618-621)
For hand-authored docs there is no regeneration command — the recovery action is 'ensure the docs were committed' or 're-run the plan', not a programmatic regeneration
Plan says 'success for every registered guide, unknown-name → unknown-id, missing-file → missing-file' without distinguishing the recovery hint from the existing criterion missing-file path
findings.finding: The plan's Testing section specifies `missing-file → missing-file` as an error state for `explain_guide`, but the `missing-file` recovery hint in the existing `ExplainError` is a `regenerate-criteria-guide` command that is specific to the generated guide — a prose hand-authored doc has no equivalent regeneration step. The plan never defines what recovery message (if any) `explain_guide` should emit in its `missing-file` path, leaving an instruction-locality gap in the error handling spec.
scenarios: A test asserting exc.kind == 'missing-file' will pass regardless of whether the message gives useful recovery guidance
An agent calling rebar explain plan in a repo where the doc was not committed gets a confusing regenerate-criteria-guide hint instead of actionable guidance

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC1 and AC2 with at least one grep-anchored content check per doc (e.g. `grep -qi 'description template' docs/writing-a-passing-plan.md` and `grep -qi 'blocking' docs/writing-a-passing-plan.md`; analogously for AC2's required sections), so content completeness is verifiable in-session without relying solely on human reading.
evidence: AC1: 'verified by reading the file and rebar explain plan printing it (exit 0)' — exit 0 proves file exists/registered, not content
AC2: same pattern — 'rebar explain review prints it (exit 0)'
The content requirements (description template, blocking-criteria checklist, commit checklist, LLM-Review dimensions, vote responses) are named but have no grep/structural check anchoring them
findings.finding: AC1 and AC2 state specific content requirements for the new docs (description template + blocking-criteria checklist for AC1; commit checklist, LLM-Review dimensions, vote responses for AC2), but the only automated proving command (`rebar explain plan` / `rebar explain review` exiting 0) verifies only that the file is registered and readable — not that the required content sections are present. The 'reading the file' clause is an implicit human check with no anchoring pattern.
scenarios: An executor could create a stub file with placeholder text; `rebar explain plan` exits 0 and all ACs appear green while the required content sections are absent.
During review, there is no automated signal distinguishing a complete doc from an empty registered stub.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief content outline to the Approach or Scope for `docs/writing-a-passing-plan.md` that names the 'description template' and 'blocking-criteria checklist' sections, so AC1's content requirements are traceable to described work.
evidence: AC1: 'docs/writing-a-passing-plan.md exists and states the description template + the blocking-criteria checklist'

Approach describes only 'two hand-written, prose on-ramp docs' kept 'short and distinct from the generated registry' — no mention of 'description template' or 'blocking-criteria checklist' as named content elements
findings.finding: AC1 introduces specific content requirements ('description template' and 'blocking-criteria checklist') that appear nowhere in the Approach or Scope sections, creating a terminology/mapping gap: an implementer reading only the Approach would not know these specific items are required, and a reviewer reading only AC1 cannot trace them back to a described deliverable.
scenarios: An implementer writes a prose on-ramp doc that omits the description template (they have no Approach-level guidance it is required); AC1 fails at review, but the Approach gave no signal.
A reviewer cannot trace 'blocking-criteria checklist' back to any described work — the term appears only in the AC.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach (or Scope bullet for `docs/writing-a-passing-plan.md`), add: 'The doc covers: (1) the plan description template, and (2) a blocking-criteria checklist the author can self-check before submission.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a short note to AGENTS.md (or a docstring/comment in registry.py) explaining the `AUTHOR_GUIDES` dict as the extension point for adding new author-facing guides, so the pattern is discoverable without reading source.
evidence: "AGENTS.md (fix pointers; add code-review on-ramp reference)" — no mention of documenting the AUTHOR_GUIDES extension pattern
"src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py (AUTHOR_GUIDES + explain_guide)" — new lookup category, no doc target named
Absence: nothing in AC or Scope specifies that AGENTS.md (or any file) will explain how to register a new guide
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `AUTHOR_GUIDES` lookup category in registry.py — a two-tier `explain` dispatch (criterion IDs vs guide names) — but the scope for AGENTS.md only says 'fix pointers; add code-review on-ramp reference'. No spec text calls for documenting *how to add a new author guide* in AGENTS.md or a design note, leaving future contributors without a discoverable extension point.
findings.location: Scope section (AGENTS.md line) and src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py bullet
scenarios: A future contributor wants to add a third author guide (e.g., `rebar explain adr`) and cannot discover the `AUTHOR_GUIDES` dict as the extension point without reading source code
The two-tier dispatch behaviour (guide names short-circuit criterion lookup) is not obvious to someone reviewing a future `explain` failure
findings.suggested_fix: In the AGENTS.md update already in scope, add one sentence under the plan-review tooling section: 'To add a new author guide, register a name→file-path entry in `AUTHOR_GUIDES` in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py` and create the corresponding `docs/*.md` file.' Alternatively, a module-level docstring in registry.py is sufficient if AGENTS.md feels too heavy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify or collapse the new `explain_guide` / `AUTHOR_GUIDES` abstraction: confirm why a separate function is needed over a routing branch in the existing `explain_criterion` (or a thin wrapper that checks guide names first), given only 2 guide entries are registered and the same 3 surfaces + ExplainError kinds already exist.
evidence: Plan scope: 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py (AUTHOR_GUIDES + explain_guide)' — two new symbols introduced alongside existing `explain_criterion`.
registry.py line 600: `explain_criterion` already IS 'the ONE shared lookup behind `rebar explain`, the MCP `explain_criterion` tool, and the library'.

Plan scope also touches '_cli/_llm_commands.py (_explain routes guide names)' and '_mcp_reads.py (explain_criterion routes guide names)' — both surfaces already route through the existing explain_criterion; a routing branch in each is simpler than a new parallel function.
Rule-of-Three: AUTHOR_GUIDES has exactly 2 entries at launch ('plan', 'review'); no third guide is named in the plan or ACs.
ABSENCE: The plan gives no rationale for why a new `explain_guide` function is preferred over widening `explain_criterion`'s dispatch to also accept guide names.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `AUTHOR_GUIDES` registry dict and a parallel `explain_guide` function in `registry.py` to handle just 2 guide names (`plan`, `review`), alongside the already-existing `explain_criterion` which provides the identical shared-lookup pattern (same three surfaces, same `ExplainError` kinds). This creates a second lookup abstraction for 2 entries where extending the existing function's dispatch would serve the same ACs with no new abstraction.
scenarios: A reviewer reading registry.py sees two separate lookup functions (explain_criterion vs explain_guide) with overlapping error-kind contracts and no doc explaining the boundary — future contributors may add a third guide to one but not the other.
If a guide name collides with a criterion id the two separate paths give inconsistent precedence without explicit documentation.
findings.suggested_fix: In `explain_criterion`, add an `AUTHOR_GUIDES` dict mapping `'plan' → 'docs/writing-a-passing-plan.md'` and `'review' → 'docs/passing-code-review.md'`, and short-circuit at the top: `if criterion_id in AUTHOR_GUIDES: return Path(AUTHOR_GUIDES[criterion_id]).read_text(...)`. This reuses all three error states, every surface's existing routing, and all existing tests — no new function needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named test (or expand AC-1/AC-2's verification notes) that asserts the new guide docs contain their required content sections — e.g. `assert 'description template' in content` and `assert 'blocking' in content` — so CI can catch a placeholder file.
evidence: AC-1: 'verified by reading the file and `rebar explain plan` printing it (exit 0)'
Testing section: 'success for every registered guide, unknown-name → unknown-id, missing-file → missing-file'
test_plan_review_explain.py currently covers criterion ids only (F1, BOGUS); no test for guide content (description template, blocking-criteria checklist)
findings.finding: The plan names `docs/writing-a-passing-plan.md` in AC-1 as a file that 'exists and states the description template + the blocking-criteria checklist; verified by reading the file and `rebar explain plan` printing it'. The phrase 'verified by reading the file' is under-specified: the AC does not name a test (unlike AC-5 which cites the exact pytest node) that exercises this verification. The test plan in the Testing section says 'success for every registered guide' but the existing test_plan_review_explain.py tests only criterion ids (F1, etc.) — there is no specified test node that reads the new .md files for content (description template present, blocking-criteria checklist present). Without a named test, the content requirement is untestable in CI.
scenarios: A future implementer writes writing-a-passing-plan.md without the description template section — the AC's content requirement is not checked by any named test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `AUTHOR_GUIDES` value vocabulary in the plan (e.g., `{name: repo-relative-path-tuple}` mirroring `_GUIDE_RELPATH`) and specify how `explain_guide` resolves the file from a guide name and repo root.
evidence: Plan scope names 'AUTHOR_GUIDES + explain_guide' as deliverables, but no structure is given for AUTHOR_GUIDES values
Search for 'AUTHOR_GUIDES' and 'explain_guide' in src/ returns zero matches — these symbols do not yet exist
AC1 and AC2 imply keys 'plan' and 'review' but no formal mapping to file paths or content resolution is stated anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan references a new `AUTHOR_GUIDES` dict in `registry.py` as the lookup backing `rebar explain plan` / `rebar explain review`, but never defines its value vocabulary: what are the dict's values (file-path strings relative to repo root? callables? resource-path tuples?), and how does the proposed `explain_guide` function resolve the actual .md content from a guide name. An executor must guess the entire internal contract.
scenarios: Implementer must choose whether AUTHOR_GUIDES maps guide name → repo-relative path, → importlib.resources path, → a callable, or something else — all choices have different fallback behaviors when the file is missing or the repo root is unavailable
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: "`AUTHOR_GUIDES: dict[str, tuple[str, ...]] = {'plan': ('docs', 'writing-a-passing-plan.md'), 'review': ('docs', 'passing-code-review.md')}` — values are repo-relative path tuples (same pattern as `_GUIDE_RELPATH`); `explain_guide` resolves them via `config.repo_root(repo_root_path).joinpath(*path_tuple)` and raises `ExplainError('missing-file', …)` if the file is absent."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the dispatch decision rule for the two-tier explain lookup: state explicitly whether guide names are checked first (then criterion IDs), whether they are mutually exclusive by namespace (e.g., guide names are a fixed closed set checked before the open criterion-ID set), and what error is raised when a name matches neither.
evidence: Plan says '_explain routes guide names' and 'explain_criterion routes guide names' but never states the dispatch ordering or collision rule
Existing _explain (registry.py lines 384-406) calls registry.explain_criterion directly with no branching
AC3 says 'an unknown topic exits 1 with a structured unknown-id error' — using 'topic' to conflate guide names and criterion IDs without specifying their dispatch precedence
findings.finding: The plan introduces a two-tier dispatch in `_explain` (CLI) and `explain_criterion` (MCP) — routing either to `explain_guide` for author-guide names or to the existing `explain_criterion` for criterion IDs — but never specifies the dispatch protocol: does the CLI try guide names first and fall through to criterion IDs, are they dispatched by mutually exclusive name syntax, or does the CLI grow a subcommand? Without this, the interaction between guide names and criterion IDs (e.g., a future criterion named 'plan') is undefined.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py (_explain routes guide names)` and `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py (explain_criterion routes guide names)`
scenarios: A criterion id whose name collides with a future guide key ('plan', 'review') would be silently shadowed if guide names are checked first
A caller passing an unknown string to the MCP explain_criterion tool cannot know whether it failed the guide-name check or the criterion-ID check without knowing dispatch order

findings.finding: The plan adds two new top-level docs files (`docs/writing-a-passing-plan.md` and `docs/passing-code-review.md`) but does not include `docs/README.md` in scope or in the ACs. A live CI gate (`test_check_docs_index.py::test_current_repo_index_is_clean`) enforces that every top-level `docs/*.md` is linked from `docs/README.md` — adding the two new files without updating the index will break this test.
findings.location: Scope section (no docs/README.md entry) and AC bullets (no index-update requirement)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `docs/README.md` to the Scope file list and an AC line: '`docs/README.md` links both new docs so `test_current_repo_index_is_clean` passes (the docs-index completeness gate).'

findings.finding: `docs/README.md` is an unenumerated consumer: an existing CI test (`tests/unit/test_check_docs_index.py::test_current_repo_index_is_clean`) enforces that every top-level `docs/*.md` file is linked from `docs/README.md`. Adding `docs/writing-a-passing-plan.md` and `docs/passing-code-review.md` without also adding entries in `docs/README.md` will break this test.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `docs/README.md` to the Scope list and include a note that both new docs files must be linked there under an appropriate audience heading (e.g. Agent or Contributor), so the `test_current_repo_index_is_clean` CI gate continues to pass.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
